sections.section: PRINCIPLE 5 CONSISTENCY AND IMPARTIALITY
sections.summary: Principle 5 requires that all aspects of a national food control system be applied consistently and impartially and that the competent authority and participants acting in official functions be free from improper or undue influence and conflicts of interest.

sections.section: PRINCIPLE 6 RISK BASED, SCIENCE BASED AND EVIDENCE BASED DECISION MAKING
sections.summary: Principle 6 states that the competent authority should make decisions within the national food control system based on scientific information, evidence and, where appropriate, risk analysis principles.

sections.section: PRINCIPLE 7 COOPERATION AND COORDINATION BETWEEN MULTIPLE COMPETENT AUTHORITIES
sections.summary: Principle 7 requires competent authorities within a national food control system to operate cooperatively and in a coordinated manner with clearly assigned roles and responsibilities to minimize duplication or gaps and to facilitate information exchange.

sections.section: PRINCIPLE 8 PREVENTIVE MEASURES
sections.summary: Principle 8 requires a national food control system to encompass core elements of prevention, intervention and response to prevent and, when necessary, respond to food safety incidents.

sections.section:
PRINCIPLE 9
SELF ASSESSMENT AND REVIEW PROCEDURES
sections.summary: Principle 9 requires the national food control system to have the capacity and capability for continuous improvement and mechanisms to evaluate whether it can achieve its objectives.

sections.section: PRINCIPLE 10 RECOGNITION OF OTHER SYSTEMS (INCLUDING EQUIVALENCE)
sections.summary: Principle 10 instructs competent authorities to recognise that differently designed food control systems or components may meet the same objectives, to allow recognition (including equivalence) nationally and internationally within the national food control system.

sections.section: PRINCIPLE 11 LEGAL FOUNDATION
sections.summary: Principle 11 states that governments should have fundamental legal structures enabling establishment of food laws and competent authorities so they can develop, implement, maintain and enforce a national food control system.

sections.section: PRINCIPLE 12 HARMONISATION
sections.summary: Principle 12 advises competent authorities to consider Codex standards, recommendations and guidelines—and where appropriate other international intergovernmental standards—when designing and applying national food control systems to protect consumers and ensure fair trade.

sections.section: PRINCIPLE 13 RESOURCES
sections.summary: Principle 13 requires that a national food control system have sufficient resources to enable it to meet its objectives.

sections.section: SECTION 4 FRAMEWORK FOR THE DESIGN AND OPERATION OF THE NATIONAL FOOD CONTROL SYSTEM
sections.summary: The framework for design and operation states that a national food control system should be built on a country's governmental arrangements and goals, with the competent authority providing leadership, coordination, science- and risk-based requirements, stakeholder engagement and arrangements with supporting organisations, while roles of multiple authorities must be clearly defined and activities coordinated and the system designed through a logical, transparent process that systematically identifies, evaluates and controls food safety risks.

sections.section: Framework for the development of a national food control system
sections.summary: This section is a non-substantive image placeholder for the framework for the development of a national food control system.

sections.section: SECTION 4.1 POLICY SETTING
sections.summary: Section 4.1 defines policy setting as the government process for establishing goals, objectives and expected outcomes for the national food control system and requires stakeholder engagement, consideration of multiple factors (including standards, risks, structures and best practices), adoption of three system characteristics (situational awareness, pro-activity and continuous improvement) and legislation that reflects policy objectives, is commensurate with risk, clarifies roles and obligations, and grants competent authorities appropriate powers while involving stakeholders in development and dissemination.

sections.section: ## SECTION 4.2 SYSTEM DESIGN
sections.summary: Section 4.2 directs that system design must address policy objectives and Section 3 principles and consider elements such as legal/regulatory frameworks, relation to international standards and equivalence, oversight methods, enforcement, coordination, laboratory capacity, staff competence, funding, surveillance, stakeholder engagement, international harmonisation and periodic review, while using risk-based control programs and documenting the system publicly for transparency and evaluation.

sections.section: 50. Control programs
sections.summary: Paragraph 50 specifies that control programs must be risk-based and consider factors including product-specific food safety hazards, fraud risk, diverse information sources, statistics, prior control results, control effectiveness, supply-chain knowledge and epidemiological data, and that such programs should include inspection, market surveillance, sampling and analysis, record review, and take account of industry quality-assurance systems while providing for evaluation and regular review.

sections.section: 51. In the absence of risk analysis data control programs should be based on technical and scientific data developed from current knowledge and practice.
sections.summary: Paragraph 51 states that if risk analysis data are absent, control programs should be based on the best available technical and scientific knowledge and practice.

sections.section: 52. Control programs should be applied at the point or points in the production or supply chain where hazards can be most effectively or efficiently controlled taking into account the available resources and capability. Control programs amongst other things may cover, as appropriate:
sections.summary: Paragraph 52 requires control programs to be applied at the point(s) in the production or supply chain where hazards can be most effectively controlled and may cover establishments, products (raw to final), preventative controls (GAP, GMP, GHP, HACCP), distribution means, and human resources/infrastructure/confidentiality.

sections.section: 53. Control programs should be designed to include the following elements but not limited to:
sections.summary: Paragraph 53 lists essential control program elements including inspection, verification and audit (on-site), market surveillance, sampling and analysis, examination of records, documentation of observations/findings, and review of establishment-operated verification systems.

sections.section: 54. Where quality assurance systems are used by food business operators, the national food control system should take them into account where such systems relate to protecting consumer health and ensuring fair practices in the food trade. The competent authority should encourage, as appropriate, the use of  Good Laboratory Practices (GLP) 11  GAP, GMP, GHP and HACCP approach in accordance with General Principles of Food Hygiene (CAC/RCP 1-1969).
sections.summary: Paragraph 54 requires the national food control system to take into account industry quality-assurance systems where they relate to consumer health and fair trade and encourages use of GLP, GAP, GMP, GHP and HACCP consistent with the General Principles of Food Hygiene.

sections.section: 55. The system design should provide for the capability to evaluate the effectiveness of the national food control system. Verifying the effectiveness of the national food control system should be targeted at the most appropriate stages of the food chain, based on risk analysis conducted in accordance with internationally accepted methodology 12 .
sections.summary: Paragraph 55 requires system design to include capability to evaluate the national food control system's effectiveness, with verification targeted at the most appropriate stages of the food chain based on internationally accepted risk analysis methodology.

sections.section: 56. A national food control system should be  subject to regular review of results obtained so that it can be continuously improved to reflect changes in product risk, the production environment (including technology), increased scientific knowledge, and level of confidence in industry, to ensure the objective of the national food control system is met in an efficient and effective manner.
sections.summary: Paragraph 56 requires regular review of national food control system results so the system can be continuously improved to reflect changes in product risk, production environment, scientific knowledge and industry confidence to ensure objectives are met efficiently and effectively.

sections.section: ## SECTION 4.3 IMPLEMENTATION
sections.summary: Guidance for implementation of a national food control system: the competent authority should prepare a sequenced implementation plan with stakeholder engagement, provide guidance, training and operation manuals, maintain consistent control programs and accredited laboratory support, assess and monitor third-party providers, establish communication and rapid alert arrangements with partners and international bodies, conduct inspections/audits/verification and take proportionate enforcement actions (including increased monitoring or de-registration/closure for persistent non‑conformity).

sections.section: 69. Following  the  design  or  modification  of  the  national  food  control  system  the  competent  authority  should  prepare  an implementation  plan including the sequence for different elements of design suitable with their preparedness and capability.
sections.summary: After designing or modifying the national food control system the competent authority should prepare an implementation plan tailored to preparedness and capability, including priorities/timeframes, deliverables, responsibilities, resource allocation, training/operation manuals and stakeholder engagement.

sections.section: 84. A national food control system should be regularly reviewed to contribute to the systems improvement, in response to for example, control program data, non-compliances, food safety incidents, scientific research, and history of conformance, external and self-reviews of the system and changes to product risk or the production environment. Such reviews may take place at the level of system or program design or implementation as appropriate.
sections.summary: National food control systems should be regularly reviewed at system or program level to improve performance in response to control data, non‑compliances, incidents, scientific developments, conformance history, external/self‑reviews and changes in product risk or production environment.
